# Transcrição: 11-Expression of Interest

- **Fonte:** 11-Expression of Interest
- **Duração:** 10:06
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

In this next lesson, we're going to focus on the expression of interest or the EOI. The EOI indicates that a buyer is serious in their intent to purchase a business from a seller. Every lender is different, so the requirements of an EOI will vary. You'll need to approach each one separately to get that list. There will usually be some overlaps. Buyers will require a lot of the same things, but there are going to be some specific requirements unique to the company that you'll only learn by contacting them and building those relationships. In general, an EOI will often contain some of these pieces of content. Purchase price. The purchase price consideration that buyers are ready to pay on a cash-free, debt-free basis at the time of closing the deal. Valuation methodology. What the valuation is based on what assumptions are being made about the business to settle on that valuation. Due diligence. The buyer asks the seller for the opportunity to conduct satisfactory due diligence. Transaction structure. The transaction structure the buyer would like to pursue, such as how the buyer plans to pay for the company. Management retention plan. The plans for the senior management and what types of arrangements for each of those employees. Transition and support services. The level of support that the buyer expects from the seller to effectively transition management of the business. Approvals required for the transaction. If the business, for example, has a board of directors, it'll state what approvals need to happen. If it has a partnership, if there's partnership agreements, then the partnership agreements will talk about what needs to happen in order for a transaction or a purchase. So you want to make sure that you get the approvals required to do the transaction. Conduct a business. The expectation that the seller maintains a normal course of business that does not adversely affect it while the deal is in process. You do not want a seller indebting the business or agreeing to contracts that you have to live by. Transaction expenses. Any transaction expense would be met by each party on its own. These are expenses related to due diligence, negotiations, their legal costs, etc. Confidentiality. The proposal or details of the proposal should be confidential unless approved by both buyer and seller. Non-binding agreement. The EOI is not an official agreement to sign a deal. Before we begin diving deeper into expression of interest, let's review the different types of assets we can finance. Receivables financing is the most common type of financing. You can typically get up to 90% of the value of the good receivables, including sales tax. And because the loan is usually revolving, you can usually repay it using the business's cash flow. If you have a $50,000 invoice and you were able to finance 90% of it, you'd wind up

## [03:03 - 06:04]

with $45,000 of financing for the receivables. And once the business has paid back the $45,000, you can turn around and take $45,000 from the $50,000 and give $5,000 back to the business. Then when the next $50,000 invoice comes in, you can immediately refinance for the additional $45,000. It's essentially a revolving line of credit. You use it, you pay it off, you use it. There are also third parties out there that you can sell the invoices to. Then they're the ones that will be responsible for everything regarding debt collection. There also be the ones who receive everything. Now, just because we basically sell them doesn't mean we should. We want to have a little bit more control. Most of the time, we want to have control over the receivable terms and you want to be the one collecting the money. You don't want people interfering with your customer relationships and being hard on them if they're slow on a payment because that will burn the relationship of doing business with you. But maybe the business you're buying is not very good at collecting money. So that might be a solution for you to sell those invoices short term while you improve the workflow of the business. Next, there's invoice receivables financing. We've talked about how you typically don't want one customer to account for too much of your receivables. For example, maybe one client is responsible for 40 or 50% of all your receivables. So if you lose their business, maybe they shut down, default, maybe they switch to a different vendor, whatever the situation is, if you lose that business, you're going to be out of a ton of revenues. So you want to be very careful and you want to identify your customer base when you're buying a business. As a rule of thumb, you don't want any one customer to account for more than a quarter of your receivables at most a third. But you really want less than that. A healthy business has a diversified customer base and you have no particular risk with one big customer. Financiers will review that to decide how risky the loan is. Financiers will also want to look at the quality of businesses that the people that owe you have because they want to make sure that if they're qualified or disqualified from being calculated in the loan, they have to do their own due diligence on the ability for those businesses to pay you. They want the highest possible chance that they're going to get paid back on this loan. So they're looking into everything. A lender will want to look at a sample of the invoices and pick a few of them to go deep into testing if those receivables are good or not or what percentages are good. So you're gonna need to get a list of invoices from a seller so that the banks that you're gonna seek a loan from can do the due diligence that they're gonna need to do. And you're gonna need to let the seller know that this is part of what you need in the due diligence packet. They'll need a customer list and they may even want to talk to some of those customers. It will depend on the lender and how much money you're asking for. Next, let's talk about inventory financing. The financing rate on your inventory

## [06:04 - 09:07]

will be entirely dependent on what kind of inventory it is and how valuable it is and what the turn rate of your inventory is. The worst the turn rate of inventory, for example, the lower value you're gonna get because the inventory comes and goes. So there's not a long term security on it. Like receivable, inventory financing is typically a revolving facility and you can repay it from cash flow. Unlike receivable, there's additional questions that the financier is going to ask when it comes to inventory financing. They'll want to know, for example, how much they can actually sell your inventory for if there's a default because the value of your inventory may constantly be depreciating. So then there's a cost of goods sold to consider. If you sell something for $20, but it costs you $5 to produce it, they'll only give you whatever percentage of that production cost is and not the price tag. Another thing they're gonna look at is shelf life and there are some common rules for the shelf life of products. For example, the longer the shelf life for an item, the higher the percentage you're going to be able to get. The customer contract is also important. Let's say you're purchasing a business that manufactures car parts and you have a long term customer. With car parts, they have a very long shelf life, even if you have a part for an older model of car. So you'll get a higher percentage valuation based on the inventory that's in your business. On the flip side, if you are buying a grocery store, the grocery store's inventory is gonna get a much, much less valuation. Most of the inventory probably won't be suitable for financing because it goes bad. With inventory financing, the lender will analyze the loss or damage rates. They may even go out and visit some of those facilities. Again, every lender is different. There might be more stuff one will wanna look at over another. You'll wanna ask about the kinds of things each lender will want to review. Next, we'll move on to equipment and machinery. There's a couple different types of loans we'll talk about. First is just a straight loan. This is where you retain the assets and they're used as collateral. Value will depend on the age and condition of the equipment or machines. For example, if the business bought a truck for $50,000, a lender won't value the truck at $50,000. They'll probably give you some percentage of what they think they can sell the car for. So most likely you won't even receive financing for the full value of the car at the time, more like 70% or 80% of the value. Typically assets will be used as collateral and there is usually the option of a sale and a leaseback. This is good for businesses that have large machinery like bulldozers, excavators, or cranes. If you're looking at a leaseback for something like an excavator, it'll work like this. The lender buys the asset from you and then you lease it back from them. And usually the excavator won't even have to leave the property. You continue using it and you get some percentage of loan to value based on the age and condition of the equipment. If you're doing a straight loan, you just retain the assets, but they're used as collateral. You can also refinance existing fixed asset loans.

## [09:07 - 10:06]

If you're still paying something down and you finance it, you can use the financing to help pay the remaining amount down in full. If you get $150,000 as a straight loan on a bulldozer that you still owe $40,000 on, you can take that loan and use it to pay off the bulldozer in full, maybe even at a lower price. What an EOI looks like is going to vary slightly depending on the company you're working with. Make sure you're contacting your financier and lenders directly in order to learn exactly what it is that they require and keep building those relationships with sellers, referral partners, financiers, lenders, et cetera. You want to have as many contacts as you can so you have as many options as you can have. This is the whole point in this game, is having options. Now, that's it for the expression of interest.
